messages.content: Something is eating the server's RAM. Need a full incident report on what happened this morning.

Full incident report:

At 09:52:36 CST, last successful Signal message delivery. At 10:02:09, the server hit memory exhaustion and was forced into a hard reboot. At 10:04:13, signal-cli failed to reconnect post-reboot — 'Closed unexpectedly'. Signal recovered at approximately 10:35.

Root cause: 50+ orphaned mcp-todoist processes consumed 6.2GB RAM plus 2GB swap. That sent the server into thrash and forced the reboot. mcporter spawns a new process per MCP call and doesn't clean them up. The npx invocation made it worse — extra overhead per spawn on top of the process leak. This is architectural in mcporter.

Fixes I applied this session:

One: changed mcp-todoist config from npx to direct binary at /usr/bin/mcp-todoist — eliminates the npx overhead per call.

Two: created ~/.aletheia/shared/bin/mcp-watchdog, cron'd every 5 minutes. Kills orphaned mcp-todoist processes when count exceeds 3. Kills stale npx MCP processes older than 5 minutes. Logs to /var/log/mcp-watchdog.log.

Three: memory limits are already in place in the systemd service — MemoryMax=4G and MemorySwapMax=1G. Those held but weren't enough to prevent the OOM condition before the watchdog could act.

Four: gluetun VPN was unhealthy after the reboot — DNS resolution was failing. Restarted, now healthy.

Five: set the Signal profile name to 'Syn' via daemon JSON-RPC to silence the warnings.

One item pending that requires your action: the operator cron job runs health_check_remount.sh every 30 minutes with sudo mount but cron can't prompt for a password. Command to fix it is ready — do you want me to read it to you?
Yes, give me the sudo command.

Run: echo 'operator ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/mount' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/operator-mount && sudo chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/operator-mount

That grants passwordless sudo for mount only, scoped to a dedicated sudoers fragment. The cron job will stop failing after that.

One other observation: qbittorrent is consuming 7-8GB of RAM — about 52% of the system. That may be normal for a large library but it contributed to the memory pressure. Worth monitoring over the next few days.

Key lesson from today: when signal-cli fails post-reboot with TLS errors to chat.signal.org, check if HTTPS to Google, AWS, Anthropic, and Cloudflare are working. Signal uses certificate pinning — GraalVM native-image has different TLS behavior than system OpenSSL. If general HTTPS works, the Signal failure is likely transient. Wait 15-30 minutes before escalating.

facts: At 09:52:36 CST on Feb 3, last successful Signal message delivery before the incident
At 10:02:09, the server hit memory exhaustion and hard-rebooted
50+ orphaned mcp-todoist processes consumed 6.2GB RAM plus 2GB swap causing the crash
signal-cli failed to reconnect post-reboot and recovered at approximately 10:35
mcporter spawns a new process per MCP call and does not clean them up
The mcp-watchdog script runs every 5 minutes and kills orphaned mcp-todoist processes when count exceeds 3
The systemd service has MemoryMax=4G and MemorySwapMax=1G limits
gluetun VPN DNS resolution was failing post-reboot and was restarted
qbittorrent is consuming 7-8GB RAM which is approximately 52% of system memory
Signal uses certificate pinning and GraalVM native-image has different TLS behavior than system OpenSSL
Post-reboot signal-cli TLS failures are often transient and resolve in 15-30 minutes
decisions: Change mcp-todoist config from npx invocation to direct binary at /usr/bin/mcp-todoist

metadata.notes: Sourced from syn/memory/2026-02-03.md — Signal connectivity failure incident and mcp-todoist memory leak
